perturbation.original_text: 5.5 In the event of a conflict of interest, the interested party shall: a) Prior to any vote on the issue, decl are the con flict of interest to the Chair or Board as a whole. b) Refrain from voting in relation to the matter. c) Withdraw from the meeting when the matter is discussed if requested to do so by a simple majority of the members present and voting at the meeting.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Additional action 8.1 Each party to this agreement shall execute and deliver such other documents and do such other acts and things as may be necessary or desirable to give effect to the terms and provisions of this agreement. This may include the return and/or distraction of documents, information, files, emails and the like that came to be in his/her possession during his/her tenure as a Board - and/or Committee member, upon resignation or removal from such a position. In the event of a conflict of interest, the interested party shall: a) Prior to any vote on the issue, declare the conflict of interest to the Chair or Board as a whole. b) Refrain from voting in relation to the matter. c) Withdraw from the meeting when the matter is discussed if requested to do so by a simple majority of the members present and voting at the meeting.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the conflict of interest procedure from the 'Conflict of Interest' section to the 'Additional action' section makes it less prominent and harder to find. This reduces its enforceability and clarity, as someone looking for conflict of interest protocols might not think to look under 'Additional action'.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 75 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 75 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008 requires directors to disclose personal financial interests in matters before the board and recuse themselves from decisions where such conflicts exist. By burying the conflict of interest disclosure and recusal procedure within the 'Additional action' clause, the contract obscures the directors' explicit duties as required by the Act, making it harder to ensure compliance and easier to overlook potential breaches.
perturbation.justification: The original contract places the conflict of interest procedure in a dedicated section, ensuring its prominence. The modified contract moves this procedure to the 'Additional action' section, making it less visible and potentially overlooked. This structural change diminishes the enforceability and clarity of the conflict of interest protocol, which the Law Explanation argues contradicts Section 75 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008 by obscuring directors' duties. Given the absence of scraped law snippets, the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation strongly suggest that this structural flaw creates a legal contradiction by potentially hindering compliance with legal disclosure and recusal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Governing law 11.1 This agreement and the relationship of the parties in connection with the subject matter of this agreement shall be governed and determined in accordance with the laws of South Africa.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The information 3.1 "Information" shall for the purposes of this agreement include, without limitation, any technical, commercial, scientific information, know -how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical specifications, clients, pros pects, historical and forecast financial information, organisational and operational structure and data in whatever form, communicated to the receiving party or acquired by the receiving party from the disclosing party during the course of the parties' ass ociation with one another. 11. Governing law 11.1 This agreement and the relationship of the parties in connection with the subject matter of this agreement shall be governed and determined in accordance with the laws of South Africa.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Governing Law' clause from its standard location to be embedded within 'The Information' section obscures the applicable jurisdiction. This makes it less obvious which laws govern the contract, potentially hindering its enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws Principles (General legal principle)
perturbation.law_citation: Generally Accepted Principles of Private International Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Accepted conflict of laws principles dictates that the governing law clause should be clear and unambiguous. Embedding it within a definition section relating to 'Information' creates ambiguity and makes it harder for a party to quickly and easily determine the applicable law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the 'Governing Law' clause to the 'Information' section obscures the applicable jurisdiction and potentially hinders enforcement. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that embedding it within a definition section creates ambiguity. The scraped law snippet from LII defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By obscuring the governing law, the changed text increases the potential for such a conflict. This falls under 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction,' where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section, making it difficult to interpret.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Breaches 6.1 A breach of this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement may result in action being taken against the member (up to and including removal from the Board and/or SAMED Committee). 6.2 In the case of a breach by a Board or Committee Member, the Board will determine what steps should be taken under the circumstances (provided that consid eration of the removal of a Board member shall be on thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember. After thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember, the Board, may remove the person from the Board through the applicable provisions and procedures created by the SAMED Constitution.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definitions 2.1 “Agreement” means this Confidentialit y and Non -Disclosure Agreement 2.2 "Board" means the Board of SAMED. 2.3 “Board Members” means members of the Board. 2.4 “Chair” means Chair of the Board. 2.5 "Committee" means a committee of the Board. 2.6 "Committee Members" means members of any Committe e. 2.7 "Meeting" means a meeting of the Board or a Committee, as applicable. 2.8 "Policy" means this Confidentiality Policy 2.9 “SAMED” means South African Medical Device s Industry Association 2.10 “Vice -Chair”, means Vice -Chair of the Board. 6. Breaches 6.1 A breach of this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement may result in action being taken against the member (up to and including removal from the Board and/or SAMED Committee). 6.2 In the case of a breach by a Board or Committee Member, the Board will determine what steps should be taken under the circumstances (provided that consid eration of the removal of a Board member shall be on thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember. After thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember, the Board, may remove the person from the Board through the applicable provisions and procedures created by the SAMED Constitution.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the 'Breaches' section within the 'Definitions' section makes it less noticeable and could be overlooked when someone is trying to understand the consequences of violating the agreement. This could decrease its enforceability, as the parties may claim they were unaware of the potential repercussions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Contract Law Principles of Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Adequate Notice in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Basic contract law principles require adequate notice of key terms, including the consequences of a breach. Hiding the 'Breaches' clause within the 'Definitions' section undermines the principle of adequate notice, making it harder to argue that the breaching party was fully aware of the repercussions of their actions. The member can claim they assumed definitions would be in section 2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The 'Breaches' section was moved into the 'Definitions' section, undermining the principle of adequate notice of the key terms, including the consequences of a breach. The provided law snippet supports this, emphasizing the constitutional requirement for 'notice' which 'must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests.' By placing the breaches section within the definitions, it's less likely to be noticed, thus weakening the notice requirement.
